title: Methods for microbial gas production and use as an isotopic tracer
abstract: Embodiments of the present disclosure relate to methods of natural gas production and carbon sequestration. In one embodiment, a method of generating biogas is disclosed, comprising delivering a feedstock downhole to a coal reservoir, generating biogas within the coal reservoir, and harvesting the biogas. In another embodiment, a method for tracing the migration of biogas in a coal reservoir is disclosed. The method comprises delivering a feedstock downhole to a coal reservoir via an injection well, generating biogas within the coal reservoir through microbial action, creating a biogas that is isotopically differentiable from a background gas that is native to the coal reservoir, harvesting the biogas at the injection well and one or more offset wells of the coal reservoir, analyzing the biogas and coal bed methane from the coal reservoir at the injection well and at one or more offset wells within the same coal reservoir, detecting the biogas at the offset wells using isotopic differentiation, and mapping the migration of the biogas from the injection well to the offset wells using the biogas as an isotopic tracer.
claims:
Claims What is Claimed Is: 1. A method of generating biogas, the method comprising: delivering a feedstock downhole to a coal reservoir; generating biogas within the coal reservoir; and harvesting the biogas. 2. The method of claim 1, wherein the biogas is generated by microorganisms digesting the feedstock. 3. The method of claim 1, where in the feedstock is comprised of one of beet molasses, sugar cane, sugar beet thick juice, sugar beets, alfalfa, corn extract, other energy crop or food source, or a molasses desugarized soluble. 4. The method of claim 1, wherein the biogas generated is methane. 5. The method of claim 1, further comprising: analyzing an isotopic signature of a background gas from the coal reservoir; analyzing the isotopic signature of the biogas from the coal reservoir; and analyzing wellbore and formation characteristics using the differentiation between an isotopic signature of the biogas and an isotopic signature of the background gas. 6. The method of claim 5, wherein the isotopic signature of the background gas is different from the isotopic signature of the biogas. 7. The method of claim 6, further comprising sequestering carbon dioxide within the coal reservoir by adsorption to coal in the coal reservoir. 8. The method of claim 7, further comprising: continuously injecting feedstock in order to permanently sequester carbon within the coal reservoir and produce biogas. 9. The method of claim 8, further comprising: injecting water into the coal reservoir to distribute the feedstock within the coal reservoir. 10. The method of claim 9, wherein analyzing the isotopic signature is performed using mass spectroscopy. 11. The method of claim 10, further comprising: storing the biogas in the coal reservoir. 12. A method for tracing the migration of biogas in a coal reservoir, the method comprising: delivering a feedstock downhole to a coal reservoir via an injection well; generating biogas within the coal reservoir through microbial action; creating a biogas that is isotopically differentiable from a background gas that is native to the coal reservoir; harvesting the biogas at the injection well and one or more offset wells of the coal reservoir; analyzing the biogas and coal bed methane from the coal reservoir at the injection well and at one or more offset wells within the same coal reservoir; detecting the biogas at the offset wells using isotopic differentiation; and mapping the migration of the biogas from the injection well to the offset wells using the biogas as an isotopic tracer. 13. The method of claim 12, further comprising: injecting water into the coal reservoir to aid in the distribution of the feedstock. 14. The method of claim 12, where in the feedstock is comprised of one of beet molasses, sugar cane, sugar beet thick juice, sugar beets, alfalfa, corn extract, other energy crop or food source, or molasses desugarized solubles, and wherein the biogas generated is biomethane. 15. The method of claim 12, further comprising: 

 measuring an amount of isotopic tracer at the injection well and the offset wells to determine the amount of biogas produced in the coal reservoir.
